01a_quotes.title: Bringing Everyone to the Table: An Experimental Study of LLM-Facilitated Group Decision Making

study_design: We present a pre-registered randomized experiment with 1,475 participants assigned to 281 five-person groups completing a hidden profile task—selecting an optimal city for a hypothetical sporting event—under one of four facilitation conditions: no facilitation, a one-time message prompting information sharing, a human facilitator, or an LLM (GPT-4o) facilitator.
In this pre-registered experiment (AsPredicted #192061), 1,475 participants were recruited from Prolific and randomly assigned into groups of five to complete a hidden profile task.

Here, we present a pre-registered randomized experiment (AsPredicted #192061) exploring the utility of LLMs as facilitators for group decision-making, in which 281 groups of five or six human participants complete a "hidden profile" task [ 13]—a paradigm in which critical information is asymmetrically distributed among group members, requiring effective information sharing to identify the optimal solution—and are randomly assigned to complete the task either unassisted or with the help of a human facilitator or an LLM facilitator.
task: In this experiment's hidden profile task, groups were tasked with selecting one of three fictional cities—Eldoron, Myloria, and Cragnio—to host an international sporting event akin to the Olympics.

Each city was described by 10 different facts, and facts had five categories: weather, tourism, transportation, community, and a miscellaneous category for neutral information that did not plausibly influence the decision to host a sporting event.

Under full information and a unit-weight linear model (UWLM) of utility [60] which assigns a score of 1 to positive facts, 0 to neutral facts, and -1 to negative facts, Eldoron is the most favorable option with a total score of 6, followed by Myloria with a score of 2, and Cragnio with a score of 0.

However, each group member only had access to a subset of facts; facts could either be public (available to all group members), shared (available to multiple members), or private (available to only one member).

To obscure the utility of Eldoron and create the "hidden" profile, the polarity (positive/negative/neutral) and visibility (public/shared/private) of the facts were manipulated such that Myloria was the most appealing option in each individual information set, as detailed in Table 1.

During onboarding, participants are informed of the purpose and duration of the meeting, the size of their group, and the availability and type of facilitator supporting their group, if they have been assigned one.

After reviewing the instructions and completing a brief comprehension check, each group proceeds through three stages of the experiment, illustrated in Figure 1, using the chat interface illustrated in Figure 2: (1) Icebreaker: Groups begin by spending 1 minute introducing themselves to each other, using the same chat interface they will use for the main decision making task.
(2) Decision Making Discussion: After the icebreaker stage, each participant is shown their personal information set, and the group is given 10 minutes to discuss the task.
(3) Post-task Survey: After the decision making discussion, all participants (including human facilitators) complete an exit survey individually.
population: A total of 1,475 participants were recruited from Prolific through the platform's standard sample, filtered to those from the United States with self-declared fluency in English, more than 50 previous submissions to Prolific, and a submission acceptance rate greater than 95%.
Participants were compensated a flat amount of $3.75 for full completion of the study, and the median hourly compensation rate was $14.40/hr.
The study was approved by the Microsoft Research Institutional Review Board (MSR IRB; Record #10883), and all participants provided explicit consent to participate in this study.
arms: (1) No facilitation ("None", N=70 groups), in which a group completed the task without any facilitative support.

(2) A one-time message ("Message", N=71 groups), in which the group was shown a reminder that "People may have different information about what is being discussed in this meeting, so encourage everyone to share all of the relevant information they have. " This reminder was shown at the beginning of the group's deliberation period, and disappeared once the discussion began.
(3) Human facilitation ("Human", N=70 groups), in which a sixth human participant was added to the group to act as a facilitator.
(4) LLM facilitation ("LLM", N=70 groups), in which an LLM (GPT-4o) acted as a facilitator.
Neither the human nor LLM facilitator was provided with information relevant to any of the options in the hidden profile task.

Both facilitators were presented with the same core prompt to "first, make sure that everyone is heard from and shares what they know and, second, act as a scoreboard and keep track of pros and cons", and were also given the reminder presented in the "Message" facilitation condition (in the "Human" and "LLM" conditions, the message was only provided to the facilitator, not the participants).

If the group was assigned to have an LLM facilitator, the LLM sent a message to the group every 90 seconds, in addition to responding every time it was directly addressed by the group; each request sent to the LLM includes the full timestamped transcript preceding the request, as well as general information about meeting objectives, attendees, and time elapsed/remaining.

Groups were sampled from Prolific until the pre-registered count of 70 valid groups per experimental condition was met; 7 groups in each of the "None", "Message", and "LLM" and 15 groups in the "Human" treatments failed to satisfy the inclusion criteria, and one extra group was sampled from the "Message" condition as a consequence of ensuring that groups could be assigned to any treatment with equal probability.
outcome_definitions: Our primary, pre-registered analysis investigates how information "coverage", defined as the number of distinct facts mentioned at least once throughout a group's discussion (ranging from 0 to 30), varies with facilitation type.

Exploratory analyses examine additional dimensions including the density and diversity of information sharing, as well as self-reported participant attitudes toward the task, their group, and, where applicable, their facilitator.

Defining a group's collective choice as the option chosen by a majority, Eldoron (the optimal choice) was chosen by 31% of unfacilitated groups, 21% of groups presented with the one-time message, 30% of groups facilitated by a human, and 23% of groups facilitated by an LLM.
Participants were also asked, "You completed the task with [no, a human, an AI] facilitator. If you were to repeat the task, which type of facilitation would you prefer?"

Participants were individually asked to evaluate their group (A-D) and facilitator (E-H) by rating their agreement with each statement on a discrete scale of 1 ("strongly disagree") to 5 ("strongly agree").
Participants individually answered each question on a discrete scale of 1 ("very little") to 7 ("very much") for the NASA Task Load Index.
statistical_results: We find that neither the one-time message treatment (Δ = 1.32, t(139) = 1.52, p = 0.07) nor human facilitation (Δ = 0.43, t(138) = 0.51, p = 0.31) significantly increased the average number of facts shared relative to no facilitation.

However, we find that LLM facilitation increased information sharing relative to all other conditions: Δ = 2.9, t(138) = 3.58, p < 0.001 for "None"; Δ = 1.58, t(139) = 2.1, p = 0.02 for "Message"; and Δ = 2.47, t(138) = 3.37, p < 0.001 for "Human"; to contextualize the observed increase in information coverage with LLM facilitation relative to no facilitation ("LLM" vs. "None"), the effect size (Cohen's 𝑑) is 0.61, indicating a moderate-to-large practical effect of LLM facilitation on information coverage.

Despite LLM facilitators eliciting more facts from groups, we detect no significant difference in the average number of overall messages sent by the group members (excluding the facilitators) between treatments (F(3,277) = 0.46, p = 0.71), and find that LLM-facilitated discussions are more information dense than unfacilitated discussions on average, where density is defined as the ratio of covered facts to the number of non-facilitator messages in the discussion ( Δ = 0.067, 95% CI [0.025, 0.109], t(138) = 3.17, p = 0.002; see Figure 3B).

Moreover, the information covered in LLM-facilitated groups may be more evenly distributed among the three candidate cities than in groups with no facilitation, as suggested by the decrease in the average Gini coefficient of covered facts for each city (Δ = -0.05, 95% CI [-0.091, -0.009], t(138) = -2.42, p = 0.017; Figure 3C), hinting that LLM facilitation may encourage groups to more holistically evaluate the decision at hand.

By the volume and length of their messages, human facilitators appear to adopt a more conversational style than their LLM counterparts. Human facilitators communicate more frequently, sending 9 messages to their group on average, while the LLM facilitator sends an average of 7 messages (by design, as detailed in Appendix A4; t(138) = 2.74, p = 0.01), yet the average human facilitator message is much shorter with a length of 9 words, compared to the average LLM facilitator message containing 79 words.

On average, 25% of a human facilitator's messages in a discussion directly address a group member, while LLM facilitators do so in 68% of the messages they share within a single discussion; while the LLM facilitator directly addressed a group member at least once in every group it interacted with, only 77% of human facilitators ever did so.

In parallel, LLM facilitation correlates with an increase in the minimum number of new facts (i.e., not previously covered in the group's discussion) shared by any member of the group (Δ = 0.41, 95% CI [0.145, 0.684], t(138) = 3.04, p = 0.003), but not the maximum number of new facts shared by an individual (as illustrated in Figures 4A and 4B, respectively), suggesting that the increase in covered facts is partly driven by increasing the baseline level of individual engagement with the task.
Although only 39%, 44%, and 47% of groups achieve this in the "None", "Message", and "Human" conditions respectively, 61% of LLM-facilitated groups do so (𝜒 2(3, 𝑁 = 281) = 8.10, p=0.044).

Eldoron (the optimal choice) was chosen by 31% of unfacilitated groups, 21% of groups presented with the one-time message, 30% of groups facilitated by a human, and 23% of groups facilitated by an LLM; however, these proportions are not statistically distinguishable from one another (𝜒 2(3, 𝑁 = 281) = 2.85, p = 0.42).

If groups strictly followed UWLM to make their decision (implying perfect perception of fact polarity, exact tabulation of scores based on covered information, and selecting the option with the highest score), Eldoron would have been chosen by 60% of unfacilitated groups, 48% of groups presented with the one-time message, 47% of groups facilitated by a human, and 63% of groups facilitated by an LLM.

With Myloria (the "bait") as the baseline option, the odds of choosing Eldoron (the optimal option) increased as its score increased (𝛽 = 0.395, 95% CI [0.245, 0.545], p < 0.001) and decreased with increasing Myloria score (𝛽 =−0.734, 95% CI [-0.919, -0.548], p < 0.001), while the score of Cragnio did not influence this contrast (𝛽 = 0.054, 95% CI [-0.170, 0.279], p = 0.64).

Similarly, the odds of choosing Cragnio increased as its score increased (𝛽 = 0.417, 95% CI [0.136, 0.698], p = 0.004) and decreased with increasing Myloria score (𝛽 =−0.649, 95% CI [-0.843, -0.455], p < 0.001), while the score of Eldoron did not influence this contrast (𝛽 =−0.043, 95% CI [-0.236, 0.150], p = 0.66).

In both comparisons, the magnitude of the effect of Myloria's score exceeded that of the alternative city by 86% (vs. Eldoron) and 56% (vs. Cragnio), suggesting that groups disproportionately weighted information about Myloria compared to the other cities.

The lists of facts detected by the humans and LLM annotators match exactly in 95% of the 272 test cases; all 14 cases in which the annotators differed involved a single fact that the human annotator detected as an implicit mention, while the LLM annotator did not.
ambiguity_or_caveats: Our pre-registered inclusion criteria are that each participant sends at least one message, the group collectively sends at least 10 messages, and that all participants submit the task completion code (displayed at the end of the study) on Prolific.

We note two slight deviations: We pre-registered that we would retain data from groups where every participant sent at least 1 message to the group, the group collectively sent at least 10 messages, and every participant submitted the task completion code. In a slight deviation from the last criterion, we checked that participants' submissions were accepted on Prolific by the research team, either by submitting the correct completion code or manually by the researchers, as some participants faced technical issues where, for example, the submission code did not appear at the end, or they were unable to submit the code on Prolific.

As pre-registered, these tests are one-sided, with sample size chosen based on pilot study data to achieve 70% power in detecting a difference of 2 facts shared at a significance level of 5%; all other analyses in this work are exploratory, and all subsequent tests are two-sided.

One possibility is that time spent sharing additional information came at the expense of time that would be spent discussing and analyzing the aggregated information. A second possibility is that the additional information surfaced is not useful in resolving the hidden profile. A third possibility is that the decision-making biases in effect are too overwhelming to overcome with an additional 2-3 more facts on average; as illustrated in our analysis of the effect of UWLM scores on decisions made, participant judgment may have been skewed towards Myloria.

Finally, we deliberately avoided prompting the LLM to provide a decision, to preserve its role as an aid and maximize the group's sense of ownership over the decision; an LLM prompted to more directly influence the decision-making process could potentially mitigate these biases by explicitly highlighting critical information, or possibly introduce new biases by making direct recommendations based on surfaced information.

While our study demonstrates noteworthy dynamics of group-AI interaction and the promise of LLMs as facilitators in group discussions, our results are likely contingent on the design of our experiment.

Beyond the implementation of the LLM facilitator and its interface, as discussed in Section 5.2, contextual factors such as time pressure [37, 66], group size [67], and communication modality (chat, video, audio, face-to-face) [68–70] may significantly influence the efficacy of LLM facilitation.

The characteristics of the hidden profile task itself further underscore the need for systematic exploration. Key task-related factors include the domain (e.g., neutral settings such as fictional cities vs. potentially contentious topics) [71], the total information load [72], and the difficulty of discerning the hidden optimal option [73, 74].

More fundamentally, our hidden profile setting is a collaborative one; as previous research has noted, the assumptions underlying our understanding of its group dynamics (e.g. cooperation vs competition, unbiased and objective sharing of information, etc.) may not extend to other settings [75, 76].

The interface is configured to disable direct copying and pasting of the information presented in the personal brief, preserving the limitations on information sharing that make it necessary to choose which information to share, and preventing participants from easily sharing all available facts or copy-pasting the content into an external LLM to be summarized.

01a_quotes.notes: This is a pre-registered randomized controlled experiment (AsPredicted #192061) with 281 groups (1,475 participants) comparing four facilitation conditions: no facilitation, one-time message, human facilitator, and LLM (GPT-4o) facilitator. The primary outcome is information coverage (number of distinct facts mentioned). Secondary outcomes include information density, diversity, decision quality, and subjective attitudes. The paper reports that LLM facilitation significantly increased information sharing (Δ = 2.9 facts, p < 0.001 vs. no facilitation; Cohen's d = 0.61) but did not improve final decision quality (χ² = 2.85, p = 0.42). The authors release GRAIL, an open-source experimental platform, and provide a dataset of 14,343 messages from 281 groups.

01_extraction.title: Bringing Everyone to the Table: An Experimental Study of LLM-Facilitated Group Decision Making
01_extraction.study_design_description: A pre-registered randomized experiment (AsPredicted #192061) with 1,475 participants assigned to 281 five-person groups completing a hidden profile task—selecting an optimal city for a hypothetical sporting event. Groups were randomly assigned to one of four facilitation conditions: no facilitation, a one-time message prompting information sharing, a human facilitator, or an LLM (GPT-4o) facilitator. The primary outcome was information coverage (number of distinct facts mentioned); exploratory analyses examined information density, diversity, decision quality, and participant attitudes.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: Groups were tasked with selecting one of three fictional cities—Eldoron, Myloria, and Cragnio—to host an international sporting event. Each city was described by 10 different facts across five categories: weather, tourism, transportation, community, and miscellaneous. Each group member had access to only a subset of facts (public, shared, or private), with information asymmetrically distributed such that Myloria appeared most appealing in each individual's information set, obscuring the true optimal choice (Eldoron). Groups had 10 minutes to discuss the task via chat interface.
01_extraction.population_description: A total of 1,475 participants were recruited from Prolific, filtered to those from the United States with self-declared fluency in English, more than 50 previous submissions to Prolific, and a submission acceptance rate greater than 95%. Participants were compensated a flat amount of $3.75 for full completion of the study, with a median hourly compensation rate of $14.40/hr. The study was approved by the Microsoft Research Institutional Review Board (MSR IRB; Record #10883), and all participants provided explicit consent.

arms.description: Groups completed the task without any facilitative support.

Groups were shown a one-time reminder at the beginning of deliberation: "People may have different information about what is being discussed in this meeting, so encourage everyone to share all of the relevant information they have." The reminder disappeared once the discussion began.

A sixth human participant was added to the group to act as a facilitator. The facilitator was presented with a core prompt to "first, make sure that everyone is heard from and shares what they know and, second, act as a scoreboard and keep track of pros and cons", and was also given the reminder presented in the "Message" condition. The facilitator was not provided with information relevant to any of the options in the hidden profile task.

An LLM (GPT-4o) acted as a facilitator. The LLM was presented with the same core prompt as the human facilitator and was given the reminder from the "Message" condition. The LLM sent a message to the group every 90 seconds, in addition to responding when directly addressed; each request included the full timestamped transcript, general information about meeting objectives, attendees, and time elapsed/remaining. The LLM was not provided with information relevant to any of the options in the hidden profile task.

outcomes.outcome_description: The number of distinct facts mentioned at least once throughout a group's discussion, ranging from 0 to 30.
The ratio of covered facts to the number of non-facilitator messages in the discussion.
A measure of the evenness of information distribution among the three candidate cities, with lower values indicating more even distribution.
The minimum number of new facts (not previously covered in the group's discussion) shared by any member of the group.
The maximum number of new facts (not previously covered in the group's discussion) shared by any individual member.
Whether all group members shared at least one new fact during the discussion.
The proportion of groups that chose Eldoron (the optimal choice) as their collective decision, defined as the option chosen by a majority.
The average number of overall messages sent by group members, excluding facilitators.
The average number of messages sent by the facilitator during the discussion.
The average length of facilitator messages.
The proportion of facilitator messages that directly address a group member.

On average, 25% of a human facilitator's messages in a discussion directly address a group member, while LLM facilitators do so in 68% of the messages they share within a single discussion; while the LLM facilitator directly addressed a group member at least once in every group it interacted with, only 77% of human facilitators ever did so.

In parallel, LLM facilitation correlates with an increase in the minimum number of new facts (i.e., not previously covered in the group's discussion) shared by any member of the group (Δ = 0.41, 95% CI [0.145, 0.684], t(138) = 3.04, p = 0.003), but not the maximum number of new facts shared by an individual (as illustrated in Figures 4A and 4B, respectively), suggesting that the increase in covered facts is partly driven by increasing the baseline level of individual engagement with the task.
Although only 39%, 44%, and 47% of groups achieve this in the "None", "Message", and "Human" conditions respectively, 61% of LLM-facilitated groups do so (χ²(3, N = 281) = 8.10, p=0.044).

Eldoron (the optimal choice) was chosen by 31% of unfacilitated groups, 21% of groups presented with the one-time message, 30% of groups facilitated by a human, and 23% of groups facilitated by an LLM; however, these proportions are not statistically distinguishable from one another (χ²(3, N = 281) = 2.85, p = 0.42).

01_extraction.notes: This is a pre-registered randomized controlled experiment (AsPredicted #192061). The study had 281 groups total, with 70 groups in the "None", "Human", and "LLM" conditions, and 71 groups in the "Message" condition. The paper notes that 7 groups in each of the "None", "Message", and "LLM" conditions and 15 groups in the "Human" condition failed to satisfy pre-registered inclusion criteria (each participant sends at least one message, the group collectively sends at least 10 messages, and all participants submit the task completion code). The primary outcome was information coverage, which was significantly increased by LLM facilitation (Cohen's d = 0.61, a moderate-to-large effect). However, LLM facilitation did not improve the final decision quality (optimal choice selection), with no significant difference across conditions (p = 0.42). The paper notes that the results are likely contingent on the experimental design, including factors such as time pressure, group size, communication modality, task domain, information load, and the collaborative nature of the hidden profile task. The authors deliberately avoided prompting the LLM to provide a decision to preserve its role as an aid and maximize the group's sense of ownership.

02_method.rationale: The study is described as 'A pre-registered randomized experiment' where 'Groups were randomly assigned to one of four facilitation conditions: no facilitation, a one-time message prompting information sharing, a human facilitator, or an LLM (GPT-4o) facilitator.' This is random assignment to AI vs. no-AI conditions conducted online via Prolific, not in a controlled lab or real work setting.

per_outcome.operationalization: Count of distinct facts (out of 30 total) mentioned at least once in the group's chat discussion
Ratio of covered facts to total non-facilitator messages sent during discussion
Gini coefficient measuring evenness of fact distribution across the three candidate cities in discussion
Minimum count of previously unmentioned facts contributed by any single group member
Maximum count of previously unmentioned facts contributed by any single group member
Proportion of groups where every member contributed at least one new fact to the discussion
Proportion of groups selecting Eldoron (the objectively optimal city) as their majority decision
Average count of messages sent by group members, excluding facilitator messages; measures engagement/activity level
Average count of messages sent by the AI facilitator; measures intervention intensity rather than task output
Average word count per facilitator message; measures intervention characteristics rather than task output
Proportion of facilitator messages addressing a specific group member; measures intervention style rather than task output

04_onet.rationale: The participants' substantive task was 'selecting one of three fictional cities...to host an international sporting event' based on distributed information requiring collaborative analysis and decision-making. This is a clear instance of group problem-solving and decision-making work. The AI treatment (chat interface) is merely the medium, not the task itself. The participants are doing decision-making work, not software development, data analysis, or any occupation-specific role.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: llm_facilitator is the only AI arm (GPT-4o). no_facilitation is the human-only baseline with no facilitative support.
llm_extracted.notes: p-value reported as 'p < 0.001' (inequality), so reported_p_value set to null per instructions. Effect size Cohen's d = 0.61 explicitly reported for this comparison. Mean difference Δ = 2.9 facts (AI higher). Both Level C (t-statistic) and Level E (Cohen's d) data available.
source_quotes: However, we find that LLM facilitation increased information sharing relative to all other conditions: Δ = 2.9, t(138) = 3.58, p < 0.001 for "None"; Δ = 1.58, t(139) = 2.1, p = 0.02 for "Message"; and Δ = 2.47, t(138) = 3.37, p < 0.001 for "Human"; to contextualize the observed increase in information coverage with LLM facilitation relative to no facilitation ("LLM" vs. "None"), the effect size (Cohen's d) is 0.61, indicating a moderate-to-large practical effect of LLM facilitation on information coverage.
The number of distinct facts mentioned at least once throughout a group's discussion, ranging from 0 to 30.

computed.notes: p-value reported as 'p < 0.001' (inequality), so reported_p_value set to null per instructions. Effect size Cohen's d = 0.61 explicitly reported for this comparison. Mean difference Δ = 2.9 facts (AI higher). Both Level C (t-statistic) and Level E (Cohen's d) data available.
